meta.title: Trump Immigration Policy & Economic Impact — Interactive Study
meta.purpose: Test (not assume) how much Trump-era immigration policy affects the US economy. Must surface effects in either direction, including null/positive.
comparisonRules: Never compare raw totals: Term 1 is 4 full years, Term 2 is partial (~16 months as of May 2026). Annualize or align to same-month-of-term.
COVID (2020) is a confounder large enough to distort migration AND economic series. Treat explicitly.
Every figure carries inline source (agency, edition, data-as-of). Unsourceable figures marked estimated/unavailable.
Economic section: no single 'damage' number. Each measure carries a visible attribution-strength rating + confounders.
meta.verificationStatus: Reviewed; gaps filled and two data-integrity corrections applied (travel exports, Cuba encounters).

term1.summary: Three iterations restricting entry of nationals from designated, mostly Muslim-majority countries, on vetting/national-security grounds.
term1.headline: 3 iterations; 7→6→country-specific; upheld by Supreme Court 2018

events.name: EO 13769 (Travel Ban 1.0)
events.detail: 90-day entry suspension for 7 countries (Iran, Iraq, Libya, Somalia, Sudan, Syria, Yemen); 120-day USRAP suspension; indefinite Syrian refugee ban.

events.name: Proclamation 9645 (Travel Ban 3.0)
events.detail: Indefinite, country-specific restrictions varying by visa type; Iran, Libya, Syria, Yemen, Somalia, Chad (later removed), plus North Korea and certain Venezuelan officials.

litigation.case: Trump v. Hawaii, 585 U.S. ___ (138 S. Ct. 2392)
litigation.outcome: UPHELD Proclamation 9645, 5-4. Rational-basis review; executive deference under 212(f); rejected INA and Establishment Clause challenges. Controlling precedent for the 2025 bans.

term2.summary: Revived and then sharply expanded entry-restriction regime, structured around full vs partial restrictions per country.
term2.headline: 19 countries (Jun 2025) → 19 full + 20 partial + Palestinian travel-doc holders (Jan 2026)
term2.trend: In effect and expanding; no successful challenge found as of May 2026

events.name: June 2025 Travel Ban Proclamation
events.detail: Full suspension for 12 countries (Afghanistan, Burma/Myanmar, Chad, Rep. of the Congo, Equatorial Guinea, Eritrea, Haiti, Iran, Libya, Somalia, Sudan, Yemen). Partial restriction for 7 (Burundi, Cuba, Laos, Sierra Leone, Togo, Turkmenistan, Venezuela).

events.name: Proclamation 10998 (December 2025 Expansion)
events.detail: Expanded to 19 fully-restricted countries + Palestinian Authority travel-document holders, and 20 partially-restricted countries (= 39 country-level designations). Cite Federal Register text directly; secondary summaries vary (36/39) by how PA holders are counted.

term2.partialNote: Partial = entry suspended for immigrants + B-1/B-2/F/M/J nonimmigrants; consular officers reduce validity on other NIV categories. Turkmenistan exception: immigrants only.

litigation.outcome: As of May 2026 no active injunction or ruling blocking the 2025 proclamations. Consistent with Trump v. Hawaii deference. Criticism documented (NPR, JURIST, Am. Immigration Council) but no court block.

refugeeCeilings.summary: Annual refugee admission ceiling (Presidential Determination) vs actual admissions.
refugeeCeilings.metricLabel: Refugee ceiling vs actual admissions, by fiscal year

series.note: Lowest in USRAP history at the time
New record low; allocated primarily to white South African Afrikaners. Actual in progress (UNAVAILABLE).

source.agency: State Dept Presidential Determinations / Global Refuge / MPI / CRS R47399

term2.detail: Realigning USRAP — suspended refugee admissions; processing/funding/travel halted.

litigation.outcome: Ninth Circuit largely REVERSED district PIs: government must continue funding domestic resettlement services but need NOT resume processing refugees approved before the ban. Case open; Apr 7 2026 plaintiffs moved to amend (alleged Afrikaner-preference discrimination).

h1bDenialRates.summary: H-1B petition denial rate for initial (new) employment, by fiscal year. Shows the Term-1 rise and post-litigation fall.

series.note: Q1 only; full-year fell to ~13% after ITServe Alliance v. Cuccinelli settlement forced rescission of restrictive memos

term2.name: H-1B $100,000 fee Proclamation
term2.detail: New $100,000 fee on new H-1B petitions for workers outside the US.

litigation.case: U.S. Chamber of Commerce + AAU; 20-state AG coalition; advocacy orgs (N.D. Cal.)
litigation.outcome: U.S. District Judge Beryl A. Howell UPHELD the fee as within 212(f) delegation. Appeal launched. Fee in effect, on appeal.

source.agency: Global Immigration Blog / Bloomberg Law / CA DOJ

h2b.detail: FY2026 supplemental initially announced 35,000 (Dec 31 2025) then final rule (Feb 3 2026) authorized up to 64,716 (44,716 returning workers + 20,000 allocation).
h2b.rule: H-2 modernization rule (eff. Jan 17 2025): removed designated-country limit; new authority to deny petitioners with labor-law violations.

certifiedPositions.note: Ag guest-worker program GREW +3.5% even as F-1 issuances fell ~28% — a divergence worth noting.

h2a.detail: New AEWR wage methodology for job orders posted after Sep 30 2025; Jan 17 2025 modernization rule applies.

source.agency: DOL OFLC Selected Statistics FY2025 Q4

term2.name: F-1/M-1/J-1 vetting
term2.detail: State Dept paused new student/exchange visa interview scheduling (May 27 2025); resumed June 2025 with mandatory social-media vetting (accounts must be public). 73% of surveyed institutions anticipated a fall-2025 international-enrollment decline.

studentVisaIssuances.summary: F-1 student visas issued at consulates abroad, by fiscal year (issuances, not admissions or enrollment). FY2025 preliminary (summed from 12 monthly NIV reports; method validated to within 0.06% on FY2024).

series.note: Preliminary; -28.2% vs FY2024. Summer (Jun-Aug) -40%, coinciding with the May 2025 interview pause + social-media vetting.

source.agency: State Dept Visa Office (Report of the Visa Office Table XV(B) for FY21-24; monthly NIV issuance reports for FY25)

pause.detail: State Dept paused ALL diversity-visa issuances (applicants may still apply/interview; no DVs issued; previously issued not revoked) pending review of DV vetting.

term1.name: Public Charge Final Rule (DHS 2019)
term1.detail: Set Oct 15, 2019; enjoined by 5 district courts; implemented Feb 24, 2020; government abandoned defense Mar 9, 2021, rule vacated.

publicCharge.covidProclamations: Proc. 10014 (Apr 22 2020, immigrant visas); Proc. 10052 (Jun 22 2020, H-1B/H-2B/L-1/J-1) through Dec 31 2020. NAM v. Trump enjoined only for plaintiff orgs.

term1.summary: Border-wall construction; mileage disputed by counting method (new vs replacement).
term1.headline: ~69 mi new wall (GAO); ~458 total mi incl. replacement (CBP)
term1.trend: Built largely via emergency fund diversions; diversions later ruled unlawful/settled

miles.note: Majority (~351 mi primary + 22 mi secondary) was REPLACEMENT of existing barrier, not new mileage. PolitiFact stricter count: 52 mi new primary + 33 mi new secondary.

funding.mechanism: National Emergency declared Feb 15, 2019 (10 U.S.C. 2808 milcon, 284 counterdrug) + Treasury forfeiture funds.

litigation.case: Sierra Club v. Trump / California v. Trump
litigation.outcome: SCOTUS stayed injunction (construction continued). Ninth Circuit later ruled $3.6B milcon diversion unlawful. Biden terminated emergency Jan 20, 2021 (cases mooted). 2023 settlement over illegal transfers.

term2.summary: New permanent barrier construction funded by direct Congressional appropriation (not emergency diversion).
term2.headline: ~25 mi built as of Jan 2026; 80+ mi initiated; $46.5B appropriated

funding.mechanism: One Big Beautiful Bill Act, signed Jul 4, 2025 ($46.5B Smart Wall). Direct appropriation = lower litigation exposure than Term 1.
funding.contracts: Oct 2025: 10 contracts, $4.5B for 230 mi. Granite (~$70.2M, ~7 mi, TX, Mar); Fisher (~$309M, ~27 mi, AZ, Jun).

litigation.outcome: No funding-diversion litigation; funding directly appropriated.

source.agency: CBP Smart Wall Map / White House / PolitiFact / Washington Times

term2.note: DHS Sec. Mullin (May 2026) projected primary wall complete Apr-Jun 2027.

term1.summary: Interior enforcement posture: rescinded prosecutorial-discretion priorities ('anyone removable is a priority').
term1.headline: ERO admin arrests FY2017 143,470 (+30% YoY)
term1.trend: Elevated interior arrests/removals; FY2020 drop attributed partly to Title 42 reducing CBP referrals

source.agency: ICE ERO Annual Reports FY2017-FY2020 / GAO-24-106233

term2.summary: Sharp surge in interior enforcement; shift from jail-based to at-large community arrests; arrest quotas.
term2.headline: ICE arrests ~4.4x vs late Biden; >8,000/week by Jan 2026

stats.noCriminalRecordShare: Over 1 in 3 deported from detention in 2025 had no criminal record; 70.8% of those detained Apr 2026 had no criminal conviction (TRAC)
stats.fy2025InteriorArrests: Full-FY2025 ERO administrative-arrest line STILL PENDING (FY2025 ERO report not yet published). Partial FOIA data (Deportation Data Project): 217,518 total ICE arrests Jan 20–Oct 15 2025 (a partial-term window, not a fiscal-year figure). Last locked annual: FY2024 = 113,430.

source.agency: TRAC report #767 / Deportation Data Project / MPI / Axios / Washington Post

judges.note: Quotas, expanded video hearings, 'no dark courtrooms'

term2.summary: Backlog reduction via record case completions amid surge.
term2.headline: Backlog 3,288,186 (Mar 2026); down >447,000 since Jan 2025

series.note: EOIR figure at start of term (CRS alt: ~3.6M FY2024)

completions.note: Highest single-FY total in agency history

judges.note: Up from ~540 early 2024; countervailing reports of IJ firings — net contested

familySeparation.summary: Children separated under Zero Tolerance. The count rose for years as identification continued. Two figures are frequently conflated: the Ms. L. v. ICE court class (~2,737) vs the full universe since July 2017 (~5,500+).
familySeparation.headline: ~5,500+ children separated (cumulative, July 2017–2021)
familySeparation.trend: Count climbed as identification continued through 2024
familySeparation.criticalCaveat: Do NOT treat 2,737 (Ms. L. class) and ~5,500 (full universe) as interchangeable. Exact 'final' total never fixed at a round number; identification continued via the Biden Family Reunification Task Force through 2024.

timeline.counts: Revised Ms. L. class in ORR care (of 2,816 possible; 79 later found not separated)
'Thousands more' than 2,737; separations began summer 2017 before formal tracking
Total since July 2017 grew to >5,400 as pre-order separations identified
timeline.source: Court filings via ACLU; ~5,500 widely cited

timeline.counts: Biden Task Force EO-14011 working universe (scoped differently); 3,225 confirmed reunified; ~1,360 without confirmed reunification; Task Force directly reunified 795
timeline.source: DHS FRTF Interim Progress Report, Apr 22 2024

settlement.detail: Ms. L. v. ICE settlement approved (Judge Sabraw, S.D. Cal.); ~4,500-5,000 covered; bars most future separations 8 years.

source.agency: HHS OIG / DHS OIG / DOJ OIG / ACLU / court filings

detentions.summary: ICE ERO detention. Two distinct metrics: Average Daily Population (ADP, true average) vs end-of-FY/point-in-time snapshot. Excludes ORR and USMS.
detentions.headline: ADP: FY2017 38,106 → FY2021 19,461 (COVID/Title 42) → FY2025 48,404 (Sep 2025 monthly 60,317)
detentions.trend: Collapsed in COVID, then sharp rise across Term 2

adp.note: Locked actual (40,075 adult + 2,113 family), DHS FY2020 CBJ; 39,322 was a planning figure

pointInTime.note: End Term 1, depressed by COVID protocols (TRAC)

removals.note: 39,439 had a family-unit identifier. TRAC 'Taking Stock': removals post-Jan 20 2025 ~234,211, only ~7% above FY2024 despite resource surge (throughput bottleneck). MPI est. ~340,000 incl. voluntary departures.

source.agency: ICE FY2024/FY2025 Detention Statistics workbooks / ICE ERO reports / TRAC

borderCrossings.summary: CBP Southwest Land Border data — the centerpiece. Handles the metric break and the encounters-vs-apprehensions distinction.

dataIntegrity.encountersNotPeople: Encounters = interactions, not unique individuals. Title 42 re-crossings counted each time (no legal penalty); Title 8 carries penalties for repeat apprehension.
dataIntegrity.encountersVsApprehensions: SW 'total encounters' = USBP apprehensions (between POEs) + OFO inadmissibles (at POEs). Do not conflate. FY2025: 443,671 total SW encounters vs 237,538 USBP apprehensions.
dataIntegrity.metricBreak: FY2017-FY2019 = pure Title 8 apprehensions/inadmissibles. FY2020-FY2023 = blended Title 8 + Title 42. Post-May 11 2023 = Title 8 only.

recidivism.note: Recidivism jumped due to Title 42 re-crossings — inflates raw encounter counts.

dataIntegrity.plottingRule: Do NOT plot a single naive line across the break. Plot Title 8 and Title 42 as SEPARATE stacked series. Label which metric is plotted.

swAnnualTitle8vs42.metric: blended; T42 ended May 11; all-time high total
Title 8 only; SW total encounters. USBP apprehensions 237,538 (lowest since 1970)

borderCrossings.term2Note: Early FY2026 SW daily-avg apprehensions ~258/day (<11/hour), ~95% below the Feb 2021-Dec 2024 avg of ~5,110/day. Mar 2025 SW encounters collapsed to 11,011 (locked CBP) from 189,359 (Mar 2024).

source.agency: CBP Nationwide Encounters dataset (machine-readable, 'Title of Authority' column)

corroborationNotes: 237,538 = USBP SW apprehensions FY2025, confirmed exactly against CBP dataset (NOT total encounters of 443,671).
Mar 2025 ~11,017 (press release) settled to 11,011 in locked CBP dataset — materially identical.

economicImpact._note: No single damage number. Each measure carries attribution strength (Strong/Moderate/Weak) + confounders. Evidence is two-directional by design.

laborSectors.summary: Employment, wages, unfilled jobs in labor-supply-sensitive sectors vs broad economy.
laborSectors.attributionNote: Moderate for agriculture/construction in 2025 (timing of declines coincides with enforcement; high foreign-born concentration; direct survey evidence). Weak-to-Moderate for hospitality (demand-side tourism + labor-supply both operate).
confounders: General 2025 labor-market cooling (total openings down 885K YoY)

source.agency: BLS Foreign-Born Workers 2024 / USDA ERS / EPI

term2Movements.source: American Immigration Council aggregating BLS CES

term2Movements.metric: LF growth slowed to +0.2% YoY Jun 2025 (vs +1.5% Jun 2024); CES -12,000 jobs Nov 2025
AGC: 92% of firms can't fill positions; 28% affected by immigration enforcement in past 6mo. NAHB: ~$11B/yr lost to labor shortages
term2Movements.source: AGC survey / NAHB / Construction Dive / NPR

term2Movements.metric: JOLTS total openings 7.1M Nov 2025, down 885,000 YoY

laborSectors.term1Note: No clean sector-level immigration-attributable break widely documented; pre-COVID tightness attributed to general low unemployment. UNAVAILABLE as cleanly separable.

netMigration2025.note: First net-negative year in 50+ years (down from +2.2M in 2024). Independently documented by Census (Jan 2026) + Brookings/MPI. This is the real, verifiable channel — distinct from DHS's unverified 1.6M self-deport claim.

selfDeportClaim.note: DHS '1.6M self-deported' (Aug 2025, later 2.2M) is UNVERIFIED — traces to a CIS inference from CPS data; no exit survey. Only ~72,000 verified via CBP Home app / Project Homecoming (CNN, internal DHS docs Mar 2026). Center for Migration Studies estimates true self-deportations ~200,000. Report as DHS claim only.

tourism.summary: International arrivals and travel exports (foreign-visitor spending in the US). Fast-responding to travel bans / perceived hostility.
tourism.attributionStrength: Strong for travel-ban-affected countries; Moderate for broad 2025 decline
tourism.attributionNote: Strong for ban-affected-country visa/arrival collapses (direct mechanism). Moderate for broad 2025 decline (Canada drop tied substantially to tariff/political tension + currency, not only immigration).

travelExportsBEA.note: BEA travel EXPORTS (foreign visitors in US) essentially FLAT nominal (-0.3%). NOTE: the +$12.9B widely cited is travel IMPORTS (US residents abroad, +7.2%) — opposite-direction flow, not a tourism counter-signal.

ustaReal.note: U.S. Travel Association: inbound spending -2.4% real to ~$175B; 18% below 2019 (inflation-adjusted). Modeled (Tourism Economics), real dollars.

term2.reconciliation: BEA nominal flat (-0.3%) + travel-price inflation ≈ USTA's -2.4% real. The two are reconcilable. Honest read: arrivals down 5.5%, spending roughly flat-to-modestly-down — a muted near-null on dollars, clearer decline on headcount.

term1.context: 2016 travel spending ~$245B = ~1/3 of US services exports (largest single services export).
term1.trumpSlump: 2017 NTTO showed ~4% drop Jan-Aug vs 2016, but Commerce suspended/revised data; analysts concluded US lagged global growth rather than absolute slump.
term1.banAffected: Immigrant visas to 6 most-affected countries -69.9% FY2016→FY2019; nonimmigrant -84.7%; Middle East tourism ~-25% after first 2017 ban.

fdi.summary: Inward foreign direct investment. Many drivers besides immigration.
fdi.attributionNote: FDI driven by tax policy, interest rates, M&A cycles, sector valuations, tariffs, global capital flows. Immigration at most marginal. The 2024 decline should NOT be attributed to immigration policy (predates Term 2).
confounders: Fed rate path / cost of capital
fdi.fy2025: STILL UNAVAILABLE — BEA 'New FDI 2025' edition scheduled June 2026. Financial-account FDI also delayed by 2025 govt shutdown (latest ITA point: Q2 2025).

source.edition: New FDI in the US 2024 (rel. Jul 11 2025)

fiscalModeling.summary: Existing published models and realized-effect estimates (cite, do not invent counterfactuals).
fiscalModeling.attributionStrength: Strong (as model/realized estimates) — projections/scenarios, not measured certainties
confounders: Models rest on elasticity assumptions varying across modelers (CBO vs PWBM vs Brookings)
Separating immigration effects from tariffs, monetary policy, 2025 shutdown

findings.breakevenJobs: Fell to 20,000-50,000 jobs/month late 2025 (could go negative 2026)
findings.inflation: Effect 'likely small' — immigrants cut both labor supply AND demand (near-null on inflation)

models.name: CBO — Immigration Surge (2021-2026 inflow)

findings.wages: Slight downward pressure on real wages

models.note: Models an immigration INCREASE — inverse reference for Term 2 restriction.

models.name: Penn Wharton Budget Model — Mass Deportation

findings.wages: Average wage eventually -1.7%; high-skill wages fall; AUTHORIZED lower-skilled workers' wages RISE ~5% by 2034 (the positive/null-for-some channel)

models.attributionStrength: Weak as realized (assumes far larger removals than verified); Strong as projection

models.name: Peterson Institute (PIIE)
models.findings: 1.3M unauthorized workers removed → real GDP -2.7% over 2025-2028.

models.name: Dallas Fed / SF Fed
models.findings: Dallas Fed (Jul 2025): declining immigration weighs on GDP growth with little inflation impact. SF Fed (Nov 2025): immigration and labor-force demographics.

models.name: Secure Communities (2008-13) — East & Velasquez et al.
models.findings: SC reduced employment of likely-undocumented AND reduced employment + hourly wages of US-born (higher labor costs reduce job creation + lower local consumption).

proposedTrackingMeasures.summary: DISTINCT from established findings — proposed leading indicators to track impact of falling immigration going forward.
indicators: Sector-specific JOLTS job-openings rates (agriculture, construction, leisure/hospitality, food manufacturing) vs all-industry.
USDA H-2A certified positions and AEWR trends as proxy for ag labor substitution.
BLS CES sector employment MoM vs enforcement-operation timing/geography.
NTTO monthly I-94 arrivals + BEA travel-export dollars (track headcount-vs-dollars divergence).
Residential construction starts/permits + NAHB labor-shortage index.
Wage growth in high-foreign-born occupations vs native-dominated (test PWBM 'authorized low-skill wage rise' prediction).
Census net international migration + ICE detention ADP + EOIR completions as enforcement-intensity covariates.
CBP Home app verified self-deportation count as a floor on outflow (vs CPS-inferred estimates).

pushPullFactors._note: Clickable annotations on the border-crossings timeline. Direction: PUSH_UP increases US-bound migration; PULL = US attracts; SUPPRESS = reduces/deters counted crossings.

factors.name: Northern Triangle conditions
factors.magnitude: >6M food-insecure (2021); 43% of households wanted to emigrate (vs 8% two years prior)

factors.name: Cuba crisis & July 2021 protests
factors.magnitude: CBP encounters of Cuban nationals: FY2021 39,303 + FY2022 224,607 + FY2023 200,287 = 464,197 (FY21-23). (Corrected from the widely-cited ~850,000, which is unsupported by CBP encounters data for this window.)

factors.name: Migrant Protection Protocols ('Remain in Mexico')
factors.magnitude: Deterrence: asylum seekers wait in Mexico during proceedings

factors.name: Title 42 imposition
factors.magnitude: Recidivism 7%→27%; ~2.9M expulsions total over lifespan; no penalty for repeat attempts inflated encounter counts

factors.name: CBP One app launch
factors.magnitude: Channeled flows into scheduled POE appointments (out of illegal-crossing count)

factors.name: CHNV parole programs
factors.magnitude: Up to 30,000/month legal entry with sponsor; diverted from irregular crossing

factors.name: Peak SW encounters
factors.magnitude: ~249,700 SW land-border encounters (~302,034 nationwide) — all-time monthly record

factors.name: Term 2 deterrence package
factors.date: 2025-01-20/21 (CBP One shutdown, asylum suspension, MPP reinstated); 2025-03-25 (CHNV terminated)
factors.magnitude: SW encounters collapsed 189,359 (Mar 2024) → 11,011 (Mar 2025), lowest in 3+ years

openGaps: FY2025 interior administrative-arrest line still pending official release (ICE ERO FY2025 report not yet published; FOIA-based Deportation Data Project shows 217,518 arrests Jan 20-Oct 15 2025, a partial-term window).
Full-year 2025 BEA new-FDI (edition due June 2026); 2025 financial-account FDI delayed by the 2025 govt shutdown.
Verified self-deportation count beyond the ~72,000 CBP Home app figure (DHS 1.6M+ remains an unverified CPS-based inference).
